response: Most patients can eat normally once they're fully awake and the sedation has worn off. Start with light foods and fluids, and go back to your regular diet as you feel comfortable. If Dr. Shelby removed polyps or gave you specific instructions, follow those instead — they'll be in your discharge paperwork.

After the procedure you'll rest in recovery until the sedation wears off, usually under an hour. A responsible adult must drive you home. You may feel bloated or gassy for a little while, which is normal. Plan to take the rest of the day off — no driving, no work, no important decisions.

Because of the sedation, you should not drive, work, exercise heavily, fly, or make important decisions for the rest of the day. A responsible adult must drive you home. Most patients are back to normal the next day.

Mild bloating, gas, or cramping is common after a colonoscopy, and a mild sore throat can happen after an endoscopy. These usually settle within a day. But if you have severe pain, a fever, or significant bleeding, that is not routine — call our office right away, or go to the emergency room if it's severe.

Dr. Shelby will talk with you briefly after the procedure about what he saw. If biopsies were taken, a letter with the pathology results is mailed to you about two weeks later. You can also schedule a follow-up to go over everything.
